name: Inversion of Control (IoC)

x.structure: The framework / container owns the main flow and calls into application code at defined extension points ("don’t call us, we’ll call you"), rather than the application driving the framework.
antiPatterns: Inverting control where a direct call is clearer
x.scale: Framework / application level; realised via dependency injection, callbacks, events, template methods.

def: A principle where the flow of control is handed to a framework or container that calls into your code at defined points, instead of your code calling the framework directly.
problem: When application code drives every dependency and lifecycle, it is tightly coupled and hard to extend or test.
mechanism: A container/framework owns construction and the main loop, invoking application code through interfaces, callbacks, events, or template methods. Dependency injection is the most common concrete form; the application supplies behaviour, the framework decides when to run it.
limits: Indirection makes control flow harder to follow
myths: "IoC = dependency injection" — DI is one way to achieve IoC; callbacks/events/templates are others
